title: Chicken and Leek Pie
subtitle: Tender chicken thighs and sweet leeks in a creamy white sauce under puff pastry
excerpt: Chicken and leek pie is a lighter take on the chicken and mushroom version, with leeks softened in butter providing the body of the filling. The sauce is white, creamy, and well-seasoned. A puff pastry lid takes it to the table.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: chicken fricassee and white sauce methods. Leek and chicken combination documented in British regional cookery from the 18th century onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the assembled, unbaked pie for up to 3 months. Thaw overnight in the fridge and bake as normal, adding 10 minutes to the time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the filling up to two days ahead and refrigerate. Top with pastry and bake on the day.

glossaryTerms.definition: To cook vegetables gently in fat over a low heat until they soften and release their moisture, without taking on any colour. Sweating draws out sweetness and builds the base flavour of a sauce or filling.

content.text: Chicken and leek pie uses the same white sauce base as chicken and mushroom pie, but the leeks carry a different flavour: softer, sweeter, and more distinctly British. The leeks need proper time in butter to soften fully before the sauce is built around them. Rushed, they will be fibrous in the finished pie.

items.prepNote: white and pale green parts only, sliced into rounds

content.text: Season the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} boneless chicken thigh pieces. Heat a little oil in a wide pan over a medium-high heat and brown the chicken in batches for 4 to 5 minutes until golden on the outside. Transfer to a plate; it does not need to be fully cooked through at this stage.
In the same pan, melt a little butter over a low heat and
the {{leek}} leeks for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until completely soft and sweet. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the thyme leaves. Stir for 1 minute.

In a separate saucepan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter over a medium heat. Add the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 2 minutes. Gradually whisk in the {{stock-chicken}} of warm chicken stock and the {{double-cream}} of double cream. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 5 minutes until thickened. Season well. Stir in the mustard if using.
Combine the chicken, leek mixture, and sauce in one pan. Simmer gently for 10 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Taste and adjust seasoning. Cool the filling before topping.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Spoon the cooled filling into a pie dish. Roll the puff pastry to about 3 mm thick and lay over the top. Press the edges to seal, trim, and crimp. Brush with beaten egg. Cut two small slits in the centre.
Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and puffed. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Leeks are still fibrous in the finished pie
items.cause: They weren't cooked long enough before the sauce was added.
items.fix: Give the leeks a full 10 minutes over low heat until they are completely soft and silky. They should offer no resistance when pressed with a spoon.

items.symptom: Sauce is too thick after baking
items.cause: The sauce reduced further in the oven.
items.fix: Make the sauce a little looser than seems right before assembly. It should coat a spoon but flow freely when tilted.

items.cause: Filling was too hot when the pastry went on, melting the butter layers.
items.fix: Always use cold filling and cold pastry. Rest the assembled pie in the fridge for 20 minutes before baking if possible.

items.cause: Dish overfilled, or pastry edges weren't sealed firmly.
items.fix: Leave 1 to 2 cm of headroom in the dish. Press the pastry edges firmly against the rim before crimping.

content.text: Chicken and leek is a pairing that runs through Welsh and British cookery in many forms: soups, casseroles, tarts, and pies. The leek brings its own sweetness and a softer, less earthy flavour than mushrooms. This version sits at the family-dinner end of the spectrum: straightforward enough for a weeknight if the filling is made ahead, satisfying enough for a Sunday table. The white sauce holds its shape under the pastry and carries the chicken through the bake without drying.
